tasks.label: Go out to the club with the friend group

memory.summary: Mina and Jae are really pushing for tonight's plans, which clashes with my midterm prep. I need to tell them straight up that I can't go out yet.
Mina already knows I'm skipping Echo for the midterm; I just wasted my energy explaining it again instead of just stating my boundary.
I finally cut the circle. Telling Mina it's final saves my sleep and keeps our group dynamic honest without me dragging it out.
I finally drew the line with Mina and Jae. Skipping Echo is final, but I hope this boundary doesn't make them feel abandoned.
I cut the circle on Echo, but now I'm left alone with my midterm prep and a sleep debt that's growing. It feels like I just sacrificed our group dynamic for one class.
I just confirmed the boundary: skipping Echo is final for my midterm. It feels right to protect my sleep, even if it means losing this group dynamic.
They accepted my boundary, but I'm left alone with midterm prep and a growing sleep debt. It feels like I just sacrificed our group dynamic for one class.
I finally crushed that midterm prep, but now I'm alone and sleep-debt is eating me alive. My grades are up, but the group feels so far away.
Wait, I told them I was skipping Echo, not that Jae and Mina were cutting it close. This changes everything; I need to clarify my boundary immediately before they make assumptions.
I just told Mina I'm skipping Echo for my midterm, which completely changes the context. Now I need to clarify this boundary immediately before they assume something else is wrong.
I told Mina I'm skipping Echo for my midterm, but now I feel guilty about isolating the group. I need to explain this boundary immediately so they don't misunderstand my priorities.
I messed up by skipping Echo for a midterm, and now I feel guilty isolating the group. I need to clarify my boundary immediately so they don't misunderstand my priorities.

desires: Fix the boundary with Mina and Jae right now before they assume I'm ignoring them or that something is wrong with our group dynamic.
Get enough sleep tonight so I don't look exhausted tomorrow morning during the club hangout.

goals.description: Send the definitive, no-holds-barred message to Mina and Jae: 'I am skipping Echo because I have a midterm tomorrow. If you want me to reschedule, we can do it next week. No more excuses.' Then close the chat.
goals.rationale: I cut off mid-sentence in that text earlier—that's weak. My trust level with them is high (7), so if they think I'm flaky or weak because I was tired, it damages the relationship way more than the missed event does. Being assertive means cutting through the politeness immediately so they can drop the awkwardness too. No hedging allowed—I need to finish this loop tonight before bed so I don't have to drag this anxiety into the exam day.

goals.description: Finish the remaining 22% of my midterm prep block right now, then shut the laptop down hard.
goals.rationale: The mental load drops now that I've cleared the air, but I'm in impulsive mode regarding time. I don't want to sit here staring at the clock waiting for the exam to end. I'll tackle the last bit efficiently just long enough to get the guilt out of my system, then I shut it down hard so I can sleep without feeling like I abandoned everyone.

goals.description: Send a direct text to the cohort declining the Echo club invite, explicitly stating I need to study for my midterm tomorrow, and offer to reschedule for next week.
goals.rationale: My affiliation value is maxed out; letting them down without a clear reason feels like rejection. Being assertive here protects my reputation with Cohort_3 (trust: 7) by showing I value our relationship enough to be honest about my priorities, rather than ghosting or making excuses.

goals.description: Complete the remaining 40% of midterm review immediately before bed tonight to ensure I don't have that sleep debt tomorrow morning.
goals.rationale: While health matters, my planning horizon is short. I'm currently in 'impulsive' mode regarding time, so I'm deferring this until after the social boundary-setting task because resolving the conflict first reduces anxiety, allowing me to focus on the study block more effectively later.

desires: Protect my reputation with the cohort by clearly rejecting tonight's plans without making them feel rejected personally, even if it causes short-term awkwardness.
Get through this midterm prep block today before I collapse from that two-hour sleep debt tomorrow morning.
